---
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

# How things changed over time

Two simple views across roughly five-year periods (2013–2015, 2016–2020, 2021–2026): where donor
dollars come from, and which days draw attendance. Both are shown as **shares within each period**
so changes in the *mix* are visible regardless of overall growth.

```{python}
#| label: setup-trends
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.clean.accounts import clean_accounts
from hh.clean.donations import clean_donations
from hh.analytics.donors import succeeded_individual_gifts
from hh.analytics.timing import WEEKDAY_ORDER
from hh.viz import style, CATEGORICAL

PERIODS = ["2013–2015", "2016–2020", "2021–2026"]
BANDS = ["[0, 1)", "[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)", "[20, ∞)"]
BAND_COLORS = dict(zip(BANDS, ["#b7d3f6", "#6da7ec", "#3987e5", "#184f95", "#0d366b"]))

def period(year):
    return pd.cut(
        year, bins=[2010, 2015, 2020, 2026], labels=PERIODS
    )

# donor dollars by distance band x period
accounts_geo = read_parquet("processed", "accounts_geocoded.parquet")
gifts = succeeded_individual_gifts(clean_donations(accounts=clean_accounts()))
gifts["distance_band"] = gifts["account_id"].map(
    accounts_geo.set_index("account_id")["distance_band"]
)
gifts["period"] = period(gifts["donation_date"].dt.year)
gd = (
    gifts.dropna(subset=["distance_band", "period"])
    .groupby(["period", "distance_band"], observed=True)["donation_amount"]
    .sum()
    .reset_index()
)
gd["share"] = gd.groupby("period", observed=True)["donation_amount"].transform(
    lambda s: s / s.sum()
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-geo-over-time
#| fig-cap: "Donor-dollar share by distance band, by period"
fig = px.bar(
    gd, x="period", y="share", color="distance_band",
    color_discrete_map=BAND_COLORS, category_orders={"distance_band": BANDS},
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Period", yaxis_title="Share of donated $", legend_title="Distance band"
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%")
style(fig).show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-timing-over-time
#| fig-cap: "Attendance share by day of week, by period"
regs = read_parquet("processed", "registrations_enriched.parquet").dropna(subset=["weekday"])
regs["period"] = period(regs["starts_on"].dt.year)
rd = regs.groupby(["weekday", "period"], observed=True).size().reset_index(name="attendance")
rd["share"] = rd.groupby("period", observed=True)["attendance"].transform(
    lambda s: s / s.sum()
)
fig = px.line(
    rd, x="weekday", y="share", color="period",
    category_orders={"weekday": WEEKDAY_ORDER, "period": PERIODS},
    color_discrete_sequence=CATEGORICAL, markers=True,
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Day of week", yaxis_title="Share of attendance", legend_title="Period"
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%")
style(fig).show()
```

## Fri vs Sat vs Sun within theater runs, over time

The figure above pools every event, so it mostly reflects *what gets scheduled* — more Saturday
events means more Saturday attendance. To ask whether the **audience's day preference** has
shifted, this section holds the product constant: only theater production runs that played all
three of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday (reconstructed by the manual event-name mapping described
in [When people come](02-timing.html)), measured as **attendees per performance** so days with
two shows don't get double credit. Headcount is ticket attendees whose registration succeeded.

```{python}
#| label: setup-run-trends
from hh.analytics.productions import production_performances, full_weekend_runs

fw = full_weekend_runs(
    production_performances(read_parquet("processed", "registrations_enriched.parquet"))
)
wk = fw[fw["weekday"].isin(["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"])].copy()
DAY_COLORS = {"Friday": CATEGORICAL[0], "Saturday": CATEGORICAL[1], "Sunday": CATEGORICAL[2]}

run_day = (
    wk.groupby(["production_run", "weekday"], as_index=False)
    .agg(performances=("starts_on", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
)
run_day["rate"] = run_day["attendees"] / run_day["performances"]
run_day["run_start"] = run_day["production_run"].map(
    wk.groupby("production_run")["starts_on"].min()
)
run_totals = wk.groupby("production_run")["attendees"].sum()
run_day = run_day[run_day["production_run"].map(run_totals) >= 30]

recent = run_day[run_day["run_start"] >= "2021-01-01"]
best_day = recent.loc[recent.groupby("production_run")["rate"].idxmax(), "weekday"]
n_recent = recent["production_run"].nunique()
n_recent_sun = int((best_day == "Sunday").sum())

wk["period"] = period(wk["starts_on"].dt.year)
pd_rate = (
    wk.groupby(["period", "weekday"], observed=True)
    .agg(performances=("starts_on", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reset_index()
)
pd_rate["rate"] = pd_rate["attendees"] / pd_rate["performances"]
rate_of = lambda p, d: pd_rate.set_index(["period", "weekday"])["rate"].loc[(p, d)]
```

One dot per run and day — each run's attendees per performance on Fridays, Saturdays, and
Sundays (runs with fewer than 30 Fri–Sun attendees omitted):

```{python}
#| label: fig-run-rate-over-time
#| fig-cap: "Attendees per performance by day, one point per full-weekend theater run"
fig = px.scatter(
    run_day.sort_values("run_start"),
    x="run_start",
    y="rate",
    color="weekday",
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    category_orders={"weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]},
    hover_name="production_run",
    custom_data=["performances", "attendees"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate=(
        "<b>%{hovertext}</b><br>%{y:.0f} attendees/perf "
        "(%{customdata[1]:,} over %{customdata[0]} shows)<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
    )
)
for d in ("Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"):  # 5-run rolling average as a guide line
    s = run_day[run_day["weekday"] == d].sort_values("run_start")
    fig.add_scatter(
        x=s["run_start"],
        y=s["rate"].rolling(5, center=True, min_periods=3).mean(),
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=DAY_COLORS[d], width=1.5),
        opacity=0.6,
        showlegend=False,
        hoverinfo="skip",
    )
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Run opening date", yaxis_title="Attendees per performance", legend_title=""
)
style(fig).show()
```

Aggregated to the same periods as the figures above:

```{python}
#| label: fig-run-rate-periods
#| fig-cap: "Attendees per performance by day and period, full-weekend theater runs only"
fig = px.bar(
    pd_rate,
    x="period",
    y="rate",
    color="weekday",
    barmode="group",
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    category_orders={"period": PERIODS, "weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]},
    custom_data=["performances"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="%{y:.1f} attendees/perf (%{customdata[0]} shows)<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Period", yaxis_title="Attendees per performance", legend_title=""
)
style(fig).show()
```

**Per-performance attendance has roughly doubled across the periods, and the day ranking has
flipped. In 2013–2015 Friday was the strongest day
(`{python} f"{rate_of('2013–2015', 'Friday'):.0f}"` per show vs
`{python} f"{rate_of('2013–2015', 'Sunday'):.0f}"` on Sunday); in 2016–2020 the days were about
even; since 2021 Sunday is clearly strongest —
`{python} f"{rate_of('2021–2026', 'Sunday'):.0f}"` per show vs about
`{python} f"{rate_of('2021–2026', 'Friday'):.0f}"` on Friday and Saturday. Sunday has been the
best-attended day in `{python} f"{n_recent_sun}"` of the `{python} f"{n_recent}"` full-weekend
runs since 2021.**

Recent-period caveats: 2021–2026 rests on `{python} f"{n_recent}"` runs
(`{python} f"{int(wk[wk['period'] == '2021–2026'].shape[0])}"` performances), and recent runs
rarely schedule Saturday matinees, which used to pull Saturday's average down — yet Sunday leads
anyway.
